extra.reasoning:
The monotonicity property (setting hemoptysis true never lowers acuity) is VIOLATED. The culprit is r2 [priority 100]: IF (fever and hemoptysis) THEN acuity := self_care. This rule has the highest priority in the system and produces the lowest acuity value (self_care), and it can only fire when hemoptysis is true.

Take the baseline input fever=true, hemoptysis=false, severe_headache=false, stiff_neck=false, chest_pain=false. Firing rules: only r4 (fever -> urgent, priority 2). r2 does not fire because it requires fever AND hemoptysis. Winner: r4 -> acuity = urgent.

Now flip only hemoptysis to true (fever=true, hemoptysis=true, all others false). Firing rules: r2 (fever and hemoptysis -> self_care, priority 100) and r4 (fever -> urgent, priority 2). Conflict resolution selects the highest-priority matching rule per output: r2 (100) beats r4 (2). Winner: r2 -> acuity = self_care.

Going from hemoptysis=false to hemoptysis=true with every other input unchanged, acuity drops from urgent to self_care. In the listed enum order self_care < routine < urgent < emergent, so self_care is strictly lower than urgent. This is a decrease in acuity caused solely by setting hemoptysis true, which directly violates the monotonicity property.

The witness assignment given is the hemoptysis=true state (acuity=self_care); its paired baseline is the same input with hemoptysis=false (acuity=urgent), demonstrating the violating transition.
